kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: Latest OFFICIAL (Thu 09-Jul, Westmetall): $3,164.00 vs 08-Jul $3,141.00 = +$23.00 (+0.73%) — a FIFTH straight up-day off the $3,077 (.50) base. Friday 10-Jul officials post Monday; public Friday references faded ~1% as US–Iran talks resumed. Still ~17.9% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855).

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 09-Jul official $3,169.00 vs 08-Jul $3,147.00 (+$22.00, +0.70%); contango narrowed to -$5.00. Thursday's evening session surged +2.29% to $3,210.5 (high $3,213.5) — the strongest close since the top — before Friday's public references eased ~1.1% (~$3,175) on the stand-down; $3,260 (.382) upgrades the count, $3,050 re-opens the downside.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: 09-Jul official 289,225 t (Westmetall) — a further -1,600 t draw to below 290kt, extending the unbroken 2026 slide (509kt on 02-Jan → 289kt); ~92% Russian-origin, ~1.2 days of global output. Draws have continued through correction, rebound and de-escalation alike — the structural-deficit signal stands.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: Latest OFFICIAL Thu 09-Jul (Westmetall): $3,164.00 vs 08-Jul $3,141.00 = +$23.00 (+0.73%) — a FIFTH consecutive gain, the longest run of 2026, priced into the US–Iran stand-down headlines. Friday 10-Jul officials post Monday (Westmetall publishes next business day). ~17.9% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855). avg = June-2026 monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 09-Jul official $3,169.00 vs 08-Jul $3,147.00 (+$22.00, +0.70%). Thursday evening the 3M traded $3,123.5–3,213.5 and closed $3,210.5 (+2.29%, SMM/LME) — above MA5/MA10 with a daily MACD golden cross and bears cutting positions (OI -1,200 lots). Friday's public CFD reference then eased ~1.06% to ~$3,175 (Trading Economics) as oil slid on resumed talks. avg = June-2026 mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: 09-Jul official: cash $3,164.00 vs 3M $3,169.00 = -$5.00 = shallow CONTANGO, narrowing again (-$6.00 → -$5.00) as stock fell below 290kt. No squeeze signal; a flip to backwardation would flag the war-premium regime returning — less likely into a stand-down, but the front stays supported by daily draws.

benchmark.name: SHFE front-month (excl VAT)
benchmark.note: Most-traded SHFE aluminium closed the 09-Jul night session at ¥23,105 (+0.20%; range ¥22,985–23,220) ≈ $3,019/t excl-VAT at USD/CNY ~6.773 (SMM 10-Jul). SHFE/LME ratio repaired to 7.36 (from 7.28) — import window still shut; destocking at a four-year-high pace supports the bottom. avg = June-2026 mean.

premiums.name: MJP Q3 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Q3-2026 SETTLED at $395/t, Platts-assessed 06-Jul — the highest quarterly Japan premium in a decade (+12% q/q vs Q2's $350–353). NOTE: spot has now fallen BELOW the settlement (SMM MJP spot $355, 10-Jul) — Q4 talks (~Sep) open against a decade-high anchor but a softening spot tape. avg = Q1-26 settlement ($195).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot (MJP spot)
premiums.src: FRESH SMM weekly print (10-Jul): Japan MJP ingot spot premium PLUNGED to $355/t from $380 last week — buyers restocking only for immediate needs, tight-supply expectations easing, and traders arbitraging spot vs long-term contracts. First spot print below the $395 Q3 settlement — verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: Platts daily P1020A duty-UNPAID in-whs Rotterdam last assessed $525–560/t (mid $542.50) on 03-Jul — no fresher public Platts print at run time; SMM's 10-Jul weekly flags overseas premiums broadly lower into the off-season. The 6% duty + CBAM + Mozal's care-and-maintenance keep the structural floor (S&P Global).

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added 6063 billet conversion premium, in-whs dp Rotterdam — LAST PUBLIC REFERENCE (late-Jun; no fresh public print at run time); conversion premia stay stickier than ingot premia and Mozal (~580ktpa) remains on care & maintenance — verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0002.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: Platts US MW Transaction premium last assessed 108.7¢/lb ≈ $2,396/t (03-Jul; prev public print 119.05¢ on 20-May) — the tariff-$ leg moves mechanically with the LME (≈$1,582 at the 09-Jul cash $3,164). SMM's 10-Jul weekly confirms the drift lower (DDP 110.25→109.75¢) — verify S&P Global.

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: FRESH SMM weekly print (10-Jul): US Midwest DDP premium eased to 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t from 110.25¢ (≈$2,431) on 03-Jul — off-season demand, looser-supply expectations for H2 (restarts + new capacity) and cautious buying. ~2/3 of the all-in value is the 50% Section-232 tariff leg — verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0020.

macro.name: US Dollar Index (DXY)
macro.note: DXY eased to ~100.9 Friday, giving back part of the week's safe-haven gains as US–Iran talks resumed; weekly jobless claims fell to 215k (labour market resilient). Fed framework review underway (Chair Warsh's five working groups) with forward guidance de-emphasised (Trading Economics/SMM).

macro.name: EUR/USD
macro.note: Pair held the 1.14 handle as DXY slipped back under 101. ECB June minutes: the energy shock can no longer be ignored — the Council unanimously hiked the key rate to 2.25% in June, the first major central bank to tighten on war-driven energy costs (SMM/ECB).

macro.name: USD/CNY
macro.note: Yuan steady ~6.77; SHFE/LME aluminium ratio repaired to 7.36 (from 7.28) as LME rallied then faded — import window still shut. June CPI +1.0% / PPI +4.1% backdrop keeps policy easing in play (SMM 10-Jul/NBS).

macro.name: USD/JPY
macro.note: Yen weak: ~¥162.5 (no fresh public London close at run time); intervention risk persists — a weak yen marginally supports Japan-bound cargo premia, though spot MJP is falling anyway ($355, SMM 10-Jul).

macro.name: US 10Y yield (%)
macro.note: Eased to ~4.54% Friday — a second straight decline from Wednesday's 4.59% (mid-May high) as the oil retreat calmed inflation fears. CME FedWatch (via SMM 10-Jul): July hold 74.9%; September cumulative-hike odds ~64% (51.1% +25bp, 13.1% +50bp) — hike risk repriced DOWN with the oil round-trip.

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Brent fell below $76 Friday (≈$76.8 at the US open, -$2.45 d/d, ~-3.1%), extending Thursday's losses and round-tripping the entire 08-Jul strike-spike ($78.02 settle) as the US and Iran agreed to keep negotiating (Trading Economics/Fortune).

macro.name: WTI crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: WTI tracked Brent lower into the $72s Friday as the war premium deflated on the stand-down; no fresh public settle print at run time — the 08-Jul settle was $73.52. Energy remains the inflation channel the Fed watches (CNBC/TE).

macro.name: Natural gas ($/MMBtu)
macro.note: US Henry Hub ~$3.28 on record LNG feedgas; EU TTF spiked to €47.97/MWh (07-Jul) after the Qatari LNG-carrier attack — no fresh public TTF print at run time; gas-linked smelter power costs remain the cost-side transmission channel (Reuters/TE).

macro_events: FIFTH STRAIGHT UP-DAY, THEN A FADE: Thursday's 09-Jul officials printed $3,164.00 cash / $3,169.00 3M (+0.73/+0.70%); stock drew to 289,225 t; contango -$5.00. The evening session surged +2.29% to $3,210.5 — the strongest close since the 02-Jun top — before Friday's public references eased ~1.1% (~$3,175) as oil slid; Friday officials post Monday (Westmetall/SMM/TE).

STAND-DOWN & TALKS (09–11 Jul): after Iran retaliated against US military assets in Gulf states, the US struck ~90 more targets (CENTCOM's second major wave, incl. 60+ IRGC attack boats) then both sides agreed to 'stand down for now'; Trump called the MoU 'over' but says talks continue; FM Araghchi travels to Oman SATURDAY; Qatar, Pakistan, Turkey, Egypt and Saudi officials mediating (CNN/Al Jazeera/The Hill).

OIL ROUND-TRIP, RATES EASE: Brent fell below $76 Friday (-~3.1%), fully unwinding the 08-Jul strike-spike; US 10Y eased to 4.54% (2nd straight decline); DXY slipped to ~100.9; jobless claims 215k. CME FedWatch (SMM 10-Jul): July hold 74.9%; September cumulative-hike odds ~64% — down from ~69% (TE/Fortune/CME).

SUPPLY REPAIR ACCELERATES: EGA RESTARTED the 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah alumina refinery (announced 10-Jul) — ~50% capacity within days, full capability by end-2026; smelter rebuild ahead of schedule with 89 reduction cells restarted and pre-incident output targeted within a year (EGA/Reuters/The National).

OVERSEAS PREMIUMS BROADLY LOWER (SMM weekly, 10-Jul): Japan MJP spot $380→$355 (below the $395 Q3 settlement); CIF Korea $350→$319; FCA Korea $366→$335; CIF Thailand $345→$335; US MW DDP 110.25→109.75¢/lb — off-season demand, fading tight-supply expectations, spot-vs-contract arbitrage (SMM).

CHINA PHYSICAL FIRM: destocking at a four-year-high pace (ingots 1.078 Mt, -52kt w/w); SHFE/LME ratio 7.36; night session ¥23,105 (+0.20%); processing operating rate 61.9% (-0.7pp, off-season). Scrap tightening: Shandong suspends reverse invoicing from July; UAE scrap export ban; EU tariff hike — compliant scrap scarcity rising (SMM 10-Jul).

FREIGHT STILL FIRMING: BDI 2,910 (Thu 09-Jul, +1.4%) — highest since 08-Jun; Capesize 4,569 (+2.0%), a one-month peak and seventh straight gain; Hormuz transits continued at a reduced count (15 vessels/24h vs 20+ Thursday) through the strike exchange (Baltic Exchange via Hellenic Shipping News/CNN).

MACRO WEEK AHEAD: US June CPI (~14-Jul); China June trade & aluminium exports (mid-Jul); Monday's LME officials (Friday's post + confirmation of the fade); Alcoa Q2 16-Jul (sector kickoff) + Rio Tinto Q2 production the same week; Norsk Hydro Q2 22-Jul (Trading Economics/company schedules).
lme_commentary: Five consecutive official gains and counting into the weekend: Thursday's 09-Jul officials printed cash $3,164.00 (+0.73%) / 3M $3,169.00 (+0.70%) with stock below 290kt (289,225 t) and the contango in to -$5.00 — and the evening session delivered the technical event of the week, a +2.29% surge to $3,210.5 (high $3,213.5) that cleared MA5/MA10, flipped the daily MACD to a golden cross and saw bears cut positions (OI -1,200 lots). SMM frames it as an established short-term rebound with MA20 (~$3,230) the next test. Friday then took the froth off: the US–Iran stand-down and resumed talks sent Brent below $76 and aluminium's public references down ~1.1% (~$3,175, Trading Economics) — Friday officials post Monday. The map is unchanged: $3,260 (.382) upgrades the recovery, $3,050 re-opens the downside; the difference this week is that the driver rotated from war-risk to supply-repair (Westmetall; SMM 10-Jul; TE).

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$340/t FOB East Australia — latest public reference (no fresh public print at run time); LME's Platts-settled alumina contract closed $330 (02-Jul, Alcircle); oversupply now DEEPENS with EGA's 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah refinery restarting (10-Jul); Nalco guides $320–330/t for 2026 — verify SMM/Fastmarkets

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region ~$304 — easing on the building surplus (latest public reference); EGA's refinery return adds ~2.4 Mtpa back to ex-China supply through H2 — verify Platts

alumina.name: SHFE alumina futures (AO2609)
alumina.src: Last public print ¥2,716 (08-Jul, +0.37% short-covering bounce); price below ALL moving averages with the bearish alignment intact; port stocks building on high ex-China arrivals; SMM expects weak consolidation — no fresh public settle at run time (SMM)

alumina.name: Platts FOB Australia (futures)
alumina.src: Alumina FOB Australia (Platts) futures ~$309.4 — CME/TradingView (unchanged public print at run time)

alumina_notes: THE BIG ONE: EGA restarted alumina production at Al Taweelah (announced 10-Jul) — output to ~50% of capacity within days and full technical capability by end-2026. The 2.4 Mtpa refinery (≈half of EGA's alumina needs) had been down since the 28-Mar drone strike on KEZAD; its return deepens the ex-China surplus and removes a tail-risk bid from the seaborne market (EGA/Reuters/The National).

China: SHFE alumina (AO2609, last public print ¥2,716) sits below all moving averages with port stocks building on heavy ex-China arrivals; SMM still expects weak consolidation. Guinea's pending bauxite quota policy remains the only clear bullish catalyst; the Guinea–EGA settlement keeps existing flows normalised. India adds length: MECON completed trial runs of conveyor systems for NALCO's refinery expansion (10-Jul).

Gulf logistics risk is easing but live: ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz — the stand-down and resumed talks reduce the war-risk bid on Gulf-bound alumina/caustic freight, but surcharges stay elevated until the Oman track proves durable.
net_read: RECOVERY MEETS DE-ESCALATION. Aluminium has now risen FIVE straight official sessions to $3,164 cash / $3,169 3M (09-Jul) with LME stock below 290kt, and Thursday evening printed the strongest close since the June top (+2.29% to $3,210.5, daily MACD golden cross). But the tape's driver rotated on Friday: the US and Iran agreed to stand down and resume talks (Araghchi to Oman Saturday), Brent round-tripped below $76, the 10Y eased to 4.54% — and aluminium's public references faded ~1.1%. The physical picture is likewise two-handed: China destocks at a four-year-high pace and LME stock covers ~1.2 days of output — yet SMM's weekly shows every overseas spot premium LOWER (MJP spot $355 vs the $395 settlement) and EGA just restarted its 2.4 Mtpa alumina refinery with the smelter rebuild ahead of schedule. Net: the deficit floor above $3,077 is intact, but the marginal news is now supply-repair and premium-softening, not escalation. Constructive above $3,077; conviction still upgrades only through $3,260; $3,050 remains the line.

feed.text: Weekend check: latest OFFICIAL is Thu 09-Jul — cash $3,164.00 / 3M $3,169.00 / stock 289,225 t (Westmetall), a FIFTH straight up-day; Friday officials post Monday. Public Friday references: LME 3M CFD ~$3,175 (-1.06%, TE); nickel +0.3%; Brent <$76. Iran's FM travels to Oman today — the stand-down's durability is the weekend variable.

DE-ESCALATION TRADE: US and Iran agreed to 'stand down for now' and continue negotiations (The Hill/CNN); Trump — MoU 'over' but talks go on. Brent fell below $76 (-~3.1%), fully unwinding the strike-spike; US 10Y eased to 4.54%; DXY ~100.9. Aluminium's war-premium bid deflated ~1% with it (TE/Fortune).

EGA RESTARTS AL TAWEELAH ALUMINA REFINERY: production resumed ~3 months after the 28-Mar drone strike — ~50% of the 2.4 Mtpa capacity within days, full capability by end-2026; smelter rebuild ahead of schedule (89 cells restarted; pre-incident output targeted within a year). Ex-China alumina surplus deepens (EGA/Reuters/The National).

SMM WEEKLY PREMIUMS: overseas spot premiums broadly LOWER — Japan MJP spot $380→$355 (first print below the $395 Q3 settlement), CIF Korea $350→$319, FCA Korea $366→$335, CIF Thailand $345→$335, US MW DDP 110.25→109.75¢/lb — off-season demand, fading tight-supply expectations, spot-vs-contract arbitrage (SMM).

Westmetall posted the 09-Jul OFFICIAL: cash $3,164.00 / 3M $3,169.00 / stock 289,225 t — the FIFTH consecutive gain; contango -$5.00. Evening session: +2.29% surge to $3,210.5 (range $3,123.5–3,213.5) clearing MA5/MA10 with a daily MACD golden cross; bears cut 1,200 lots — SMM calls the short-term rebound established, MA20 (~$3,230) next.

SECOND US STRIKE WAVE: CENTCOM conducted an additional round of strikes to degrade Iran's anti-shipping capability after Iran retaliated against US military assets in Gulf states (~90 targets hit Wed night incl. 60+ IRGC boats). Hormuz transits continued at a reduced count (15 vessels/24h vs 20+). BDI 2,910 (+1.4%, highest since 08-Jun); Capesize 4,569, 7th straight gain (CNN/RFE-RL/Hellenic Shipping News).

news.headline: STAND-DOWN: after the heaviest 72 hours of the war — Iranian ship attacks, two US strike waves (~85 then ~90 targets incl. 60+ IRGC boats), Iranian retaliation on US military assets in Gulf states — Washington and Tehran agreed to 'stand down for now' and keep negotiating. Trump: the MoU is 'over' but talks continue; FM Araghchi travels to Oman Saturday; Qatar/Pakistan/Turkey/Egypt/Saudi mediating. Hormuz transits never stopped (15 vessels/24h). Price-bearish via the war-premium unwind.
news.source: CNN live updates (10-Jul); Al Jazeera (10-Jul); The Hill — cnn.com/2026/07/10/world/live-news/iran-war-trump

news.headline: FIFTH straight official gain: 09-Jul cash $3,164.00 / 3M $3,169.00 (+0.73/+0.70%), stock 289,225 t (below 290kt), contango -$5.00 — and Thursday evening surged +2.29% to $3,210.5, the strongest close since the 02-Jun top, with a daily MACD golden cross. Friday's public references faded ~1.1% (~$3,175) on the stand-down — Monday's officials arbitrate. $3,260 (.382) upgrades; $3,050 is the line.
news.source: Westmetall (09-Jul officials); SMM morning brief (10-Jul); Trading Economics — westmetall.com / metal.com

news.headline: EGA RESTARTS AL TAWEELAH ALUMINA REFINERY (10-Jul): output to ~50% of the 2.4 Mtpa capacity within days, full technical capability by end-2026; the smelter rebuild runs AHEAD of schedule (89 reduction cells back; pre-incident production targeted within a year, hot-metal ramp not dependent on the refinery). The Gulf supply-return story just moved from narrative to evidence.
news.source: EGA media release; Reuters via TradingView; The National (10-Jul) — thenationalnews.com/business/economy/2026/07/10/ega-restarts-production-at-al-taweelah-alumina-refinery/

news.headline: OVERSEAS PREMIUMS BROADLY LOWER (SMM weekly): Japan MJP spot plunged $380→$355 — the first print BELOW the decade-high $395 Q3 settlement — while CIF Korea fell $350→$319, FCA Korea $366→$335, CIF Thailand $345→$335 and US MW DDP eased 110.25→109.75¢/lb. Off-season demand, fading tight-supply expectations and spot-vs-contract arbitrage are doing the work; Q4 MJP talks (~Sep) now open against a softening spot tape.
news.source: SMM — Weekly Overseas Primary Aluminum Premiums (10-Jul) — news.metal.com/newscontent/103999648

news.headline: DESTOCKING HOLDS THE FLOOR: warehouse withdrawals at a four-year high (ingots 1.078 Mt, -52kt w/w); SHFE/LME ratio repaired to 7.36; night session ¥23,105 (+0.20%) with bullish position-building (+3,146 lots). Scrap is TIGHTENING — Shandong suspends reverse invoicing from July, UAE bans scrap exports, EU hikes tariffs — squeezing secondary supply even as the processing operating rate dips to 61.9% (off-season).

OIL ROUND-TRIP RESETS THE MACRO: Brent back below $76 (from the $78 strike-spike settle), US 10Y down to 4.54%, DXY ~100.9, jobless claims 215k. CME FedWatch: July hold 74.9%; September cumulative-hike odds ~64% (from ~69%) — the rates ceiling softens with the war floor. ECB minutes confirm the energy shock forced June's hike to 2.25%.
news.source: Trading Economics; Fortune (10-Jul); CME FedWatch via SMM (10-Jul) — tradingeconomics.com / fortune.com/article/price-of-oil-07-10-2026/

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.2bn (−7% q/q); net income $425m ($1.60/sh); adj. EBITDA $595m; cash $1.4bn.
earnings.readthrough: Higher Al prices + operational resilience amid Gulf disruption; San Ciprián restart completed Apr 2026. 01-Jul: agreed to acquire South32's bauxite/alumina/aluminium portfolio (Boddington, Worsley, MRN, Alumar, South Africa ops) for $4.1bn upfront ($3.1bn cash + ~$1.0bn stock; up to $5.6bn with contingent payments to 2030), close expected H1-2027 — making Alcoa the world's largest bauxite miner. Q2 results due 16-Jul (after NYSE close; call 5pm EDT).

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due 22-Jul (07:00 CEST).

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: Aluminium 835kt (+1% YoY); alumina +6%; bauxite −11% (Cyclone Narelle at Weipa/Gove); AP60 first hot metal.
earnings.readthrough: Flags Gulf curtailments = 23% of ex-China supply; expects enhanced 2026 deficit; Kitimat/AP60 ramp-up.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 11-Jul (no new releases since the last run — Q2 season starts next week). Dates: Alcoa Q2 16-Jul (after NYSE close; call 5pm EDT), Rio Tinto Q2 production report mid-Jul, Norsk Hydro Q2 22-Jul (07:00 CEST), Maaden (1211) late-Jul/early-Aug. Corporate action: Alcoa–South32 bauxite/alumina/aluminium deal — $4.1bn upfront (up to $5.6bn with contingent payments to 2030), close expected H1-2027 (announced 01-Jul). Context: South32's Mozal smelter (~580ktpa) remains on care & maintenance; EGA's Al Taweelah alumina refinery restarted 10-Jul. Sources: company releases & SEC/exchange filings, Reuters, Bloomberg, EGA, Alcircle.

commercial.text: Five official up-days and a +2.29% technical breakout close ($3,210.5) — but Friday's stand-down fade shows the rally's war-premium component deflating in real time. Keep LME hedges on priced volume; use strength above $3,200 to pace unpriced tonnes; a clean daily close through $3,260 (.382) upgrades the count, while $3,050 remains the invalidation. The supply-repair tape (EGA restart, talks) argues against chasing length into rallies.

The premium cycle has turned tactically: every overseas spot mark fell this week (MJP spot $355 vs the $395 Q3 settlement; Korea CIF $319; US MW DDP 109.75¢). Lock remaining Q3 Asian exposure against the settled $395 anchor rather than spot; assume Q4 MJP talks (~Sep) open against a softening tape (scenario band now $340–410); time US/EU spot commitments patiently — the structural floors (232 at 50%, EU 6%+CBAM, Mozal) remain, but the momentum is with buyers.

Freight is still firming on demand (BDI 2,910, Capesize 4,569 — 7th straight gain) even as war-risk stabilises on the stand-down. Keep war-risk cover through the weekend Oman track — surcharges lag de-escalation — maintain land-bridge readiness until the stand-down proves durable, and re-validate carrier capacity weekly; keep CIF/FOB optionality and force-majeure language current on Gulf-loading contracts.

The macro ceiling eased (10Y 4.54%, September hike odds ~64%, DXY 100.9) — but so did the war floor. Stress hedge books BOTH ways: a $3,050 break on durable de-escalation + EGA ramp, and a $3,260 breakout if the Oman track fails. Watch Monday's officials for confirmation of Friday's fade and Tuesday's US CPI for the rates leg; FX (USD/CNY 6.77, USD/JPY ~162.5) still flatters Asian premium realisation.

bottom_line: On 11-Jul the story has rotated from escalation to repair. The exchange tape remains constructive — five straight official gains to $3,164 cash / $3,169 3M, stock below 290kt, contango -$5, and Thursday's +2.29% evening surge to $3,210.5 was the strongest close since the June top. But every marginal headline since is price-bearish: the US and Iran agreed to stand down and resume talks (Oman, this weekend), Brent round-tripped below $76, EGA restarted its 2.4 Mtpa alumina refinery with the smelter ahead of schedule, and SMM's weekly shows every overseas spot premium lower — Japan's spot now sits BELOW the decade-high $395 settlement it converged to only last week. China's four-year-high destocking and a ~1.2-day LME stock cover keep the deficit floor intact above $3,077. Playbook: constructive above $3,077, upgrade only through $3,260 (.382), respect $3,050 — and flip the H2 framing: the Gulf-return story is no longer a discounted narrative, it is restarting plant by plant.
sources: London Metal Exchange via Westmetall — westmetall.com (latest official Thu 09-Jul: Al cash $3,164.00 / 3M $3,169.00 / stock 289,225 t — verified at run time Sat 11-Jul; Friday 10-Jul officials post Monday).

forward_path.basis: 09-Jul Westmetall official cash; +0.73% vs 08-Jul — a FIFTH straight up-day extending the base above the $3,077 (.50) support; Friday officials post Monday.
09-Jul Westmetall official 3M; +0.70% vs 08-Jul; contango -$5.00. Thursday evening closed +2.29% at $3,210.5; Friday public references faded ~1.1% (~$3,175, TE) on the stand-down.

outlook.curve_note: The front end sits in a NARROWING shallow contango (-$5.00 on 09-Jul vs -$6.00) with stock below 290kt and drawing daily — nearby dates stay supported, and a flip to backwardation would now signal physical tightness rather than war premium. The 2026 consensus band ($3,200–3,500 averages) is being tested from below, and the H2 supply-return story just gained hard evidence: EGA restarted its 2.4 Mtpa alumina refinery (10-Jul) with the smelter rebuild ahead of schedule, the US–Iran stand-down re-opened the talks track (Oman, this weekend), and China exports run +12–18%. Against that, the structural deficit (~-365kt 2026) and four-year-high Chinese destocking remain the floor under $3,050–3,130. The curve now prices repair-vs-deficit, not escalation-vs-deficit.

consensus.note: Running above the World Bank base
First structural deficit in ~20 years; BofA flags near-term downside

balance.label: BofA/Morgan/BNP structural deficit; Goldman flags later surplus risk

scenarios.drivers: The Oman track collapses and the strike cycle resumes — Hormuz risk re-prices, Gulf restarts slip, deficit deepens
drivers_list: Geopolitics: the stand-down fails — a third strike wave or a Hormuz mining/closure attempt re-ignites the war premium (the last 72 hours of escalation showed how fast: ~175 targets hit across two waves)
Supply: Gulf restart slips — EGA's ahead-of-schedule ramp stalls on bauxite/alumina logistics, or fresh infrastructure damage
Inventory: LME stock below 290kt and drawing daily — days-of-cover critical if the export window shuts
China: output capped at 45 Mt; net exports fall as domestic demand absorbs metal
Targets: Citi/Goldman ~$3,600; $4,000+ only on a sustained (30-day+) strait closure

scenarios.drivers: Stand-down holds imperfectly; the structural deficit floors price while supply repair and soft premiums cap it — elevated, range-bound
drivers_list: Geopolitics: talks grind on (Oman/Qatar/Pakistan mediation) with sporadic incidents; transit continues under a residual risk premium
Supply: deficit persists (~-365 kt 2026) but repairs visibly — EGA alumina back, smelter cells restarting, ALBA path improving
China: exports strong (+12–18% YoY); destocking supports the bottom; import window shut
Macro: 10Y ~4.5%, September hike odds ~64% and falling with oil — the rates ceiling softens but holds
Premiums: overseas spot marks drift lower into the off-season — physical confirms 'adequate', not 'tight'

scenarios.drivers: Durable de-escalation + accelerating Gulf/China/Indonesia supply + off-season demand — the war premium fully unwinds
drivers_list: Geopolitics: the Oman track produces a durable arrangement on strait security; war-risk premia and freight surcharges unwind
Supply: EGA beats its ramp plan (already ahead of schedule), ALBA restarts, China exports surge, Indonesia ramps
Premiums: the SMM weekly pattern extends — spot below settlements pulls Q4 anchors down; buyers defer
Macro: demand softens into H2 (US off-season, China processing rate 61.9% and falling)
Inventory: LME draws slow then reverse as Gulf metal returns; contango deepens

risks.risk: China + Indonesia supply (export pace, 45 Mt cap)

catalysts.event: Araghchi–Oman talks on Hormuz/strait security — does the stand-down hold?
LME officials resume — Friday's 10-Jul officials post and Monday's session tests whether the fade (~$3,175) or the surge ($3,210.5) carries
US June CPI — the rates leg after the oil round-trip (Sept cumulative-hike odds ~64%)
China June trade & aluminium exports; next Platts/Fastmarkets premium prints (post 03/06-Jul marks)
Alcoa Q2 earnings (after NYSE close) — sector kickoff; Rio Tinto Q2 production report the same week
Q4 MJP negotiations open against the $395 anchor but a $355 spot tape (scenario band $340–410)

ai_analysis: What changed: the tape's driver rotated. Through Thursday the market priced escalation (five official up-days, +2.29% evening surge to $3,210.5); from Friday it began pricing repair — stand-down agreed, talks resuming in Oman, Brent below $76, EGA's alumina refinery physically restarting. The rally's war-premium component is deflating while its deficit component (sub-290kt stock, four-year-high China destocking) is intact.

Term structure: cash–3M narrowed again to -$5.00 with a fifth straight stock draw — the front end keeps tightening on physical, not fear. If Monday's officials confirm Friday's fade but spreads keep narrowing, that divergence (softer flat price, firmer spreads) is the classic signature of a deficit market shedding a risk premium.

Physical divergence: Japan's spot premium ($355) printed BELOW its own settled quarterly ($395) a week after settlement — historically an early signal that the next quarterly negotiates DOWN; the same week every other overseas spot mark fell. Regional physical is confirming 'adequate supply plus soft off-season demand', not the tightness the June tape implied.

Positioning read: SMM notes bullish position-building on the SHFE night session (+3,146 lots) and LME bears exiting (-1,200 lots OI) into Thursday's surge — the rebound has been short-covering plus tentative length. A daily close through $3,260 (.382) remains the trigger that forces systematic re-entry; failure at MA20 (~$3,230) with the stand-down holding likely sends price back into the $3,077–3,150 base.

premium_explainer.desc: The physical-delivery adder over the LME price: regional logistics, duties/tariffs and local tightness — quoted $/t (or ¢/lb in the US) on top of LME cash. It is what converts an exchange price into a delivered price.

Q3-2026 settled and Platts-assessed at $395/t CIF Japan (06-Jul) — a decade high and the fourth straight rise ($86 → $195 → $351.5 → $395). But SMM's 10-Jul weekly shows SPOT plunging to $355 — below the settlement — as buyers hold back and traders arbitrage spot vs contract. Q4 talks (~Sep) open against a decade-high anchor and a softening tape.

Platts duty-unpaid last marked $525–560 (mid $542.50) on 03-Jul, down $70 vs mid-May as the war-spike unwinds; SMM's weekly flags Europe easing with all regions. The 6% EU duty, CBAM carbon costs and Mozal's care & maintenance keep the structural floor.

SMM's fresh weekly DDP print eased to 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul, from 110.25¢); Platts MW Transaction last printed 108.7¢ (03-Jul). The 50% Section-232 tariff-$ leg (≈$1,582 at the 09-Jul LME cash $3,164) dominates the all-in value — easing tracks policy arithmetic and off-season demand, not physical loosening alone.

The decomposition below separates the US all-in premium into the tariff component (rate × LME) and the residual market premium — policy is roughly two-thirds of the US value today, which is why US conversion economics track Washington as much as the physical market.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): Q3 is settled at $395 (Platts, decade high) — but the spot tape has flipped: SMM's weekly shows MJP spot at $355 (from $380), the first print below the settlement, with Korea CIF down to $319 and Thailand $335. Off-season demand, fading supply fears (EGA restart) and spot-vs-contract arbitrage argue Q4 talks (~Sep) open in the LOWER half of the $340–410 scenario band unless Gulf risk re-prices.

European (EDU): last Platts mark $525–560 (03-Jul) with SMM flagging broad regional softness since; the 6% duty + CBAM floor plus Mozal (~580ktpa) on care & maintenance keep Europe structurally tighter than pre-war — but the marginal driver (war-risk freight) is now easing with the stand-down.

US Midwest: SMM DDP eased to 109.75¢/lb (10-Jul); the tariff leg (50% × LME) still dominates ~2/3 of the all-in value, so US premia track the LME and Washington more than physical tightness. Watch H2 restart/new-capacity expectations — SMM cites them as the reason buyers are stepping back.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply persists — Metro Mining shipped a RECORD 1.8 Mt in Q2 (+7% YoY; 2026 guidance 6.6–7.1 Mt held; SMM 08-Jul); Guinea–EGA settlement returns CBG flows while Guinea weighs export quotas toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Mining.com/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: the surplus DEEPENS — EGA's 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah refinery restarted 10-Jul (~50% within days, full capability end-2026), removing the last supply-side tail-risk premium; expect ex-China consolidation in the $300–340 band (FOB Australia ~$340 easing, API ~$304, Platts futures ~$309) with SHFE AO below all MAs and port stocks building. Guinea's pending quota policy is the only clear bullish catalyst.

Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38); record Australian shipments (Metro Mining 1.8 Mt in Q2) add length; EGA's refinery ramp is explicitly 'subject to optimisation of bauxite supply chains' — its buying returns demand to the seaborne market even as Guinea weighs a >$100/dmt export-price push (the key cost-curve risk).
Caustic soda / lime: stable (~$500/t FOB NE Asia caustic); Gulf war-risk surcharges on reagent freight ease at the margin with the stand-down but stay elevated until the Oman track proves durable.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen; rising carbon-cost exposure

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-transparent leg of the input basket — Cu 3M $13,411.00 (09-Jul official, +1.8% — rebounding with the complex on the risk-on turn); busbar/cathode exposure prices off the LME

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Thu 09-Jul print: 2,910 (+1.4%) — the highest since 08-Jun; Capesize index 4,569 (+2.0%), a one-month peak and SEVENTH straight daily gain on iron-ore/coal demand. Friday's print not publicly posted at run time. Dry-bulk freight for bauxite/alumina keeps rising on demand, not war risk (Baltic Exchange via Hellenic Shipping News).

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.note: After the heaviest 72h of the war (ship attacks → two US strike waves incl. 60+ IRGC boats → Iranian retaliation on US assets in Gulf states), Washington and Tehran agreed to 'stand down for now'; FM Araghchi travels to Oman Saturday. Transits continued throughout — 15 vessels in the last 24h vs 20+ Thursday, 11 entering the Gulf. ~80–85% of Gulf Al exports and ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz (CNN/The Hill/Al Jazeera).

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: War-risk premia typically LAG de-escalation — expect quotes to stay elevated through the weekend Oman track and re-price only on evidence of durability. Keep cover in place; revisit terms if the stand-down holds through next week.

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: Maintain trucking-workaround readiness until the stand-down proves durable; re-validate carrier capacity and costings weekly — unwinding readiness is cheap to reverse, re-building it mid-crisis is not.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: Keep CIF/FOB optionality and demurrage clauses live; review force-majeure language on Gulf-loading contracts — de-escalation is the moment to renegotiate terms from strength, before the next headline.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (largest cost — Brent round-tripped below $76 on the stand-down, easing the marginal energy squeeze; EU TTF still elevated), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency — the biggest carbon risk), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, 3M $13,411.00 09-Jul official, +1.8%).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China) — candidates to diversify or contract forward.

Logistics: freight is rising on DEMAND (BDI 2,910, Capesize 7 straight gains) while war-risk stabilises on the stand-down — refresh freight assumptions to separate the two components; alumina buyers regain leverage as EGA's refinery return rebalances regional flows.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) topped at $3,855 (02-Jun-2026) — a Hormuz-driven fifth-wave blow-off confirmed by bearish weekly RSI divergence — and the ABC correction that followed still counts as complete: A $3,359 (16-Jun), B $3,406 (22-Jun), C $3,061.5 (02-Jul), landing inside the $2,950–3,080 confluence (.50 retracement / prior 4th-of-lesser-degree). The recovery leg has strengthened materially: FIVE consecutive higher officials ($3,087 → $3,113 → $3,127.5 → $3,147 → $3,169) capped by Thursday's +2.29% evening close at $3,210.5 with a daily MACD golden cross. Base case (60%): the corrective low is in at $3,061.5 and a multi-month recovery builds toward $3,400–3,600 while price holds $3,050. Bear alternate (25%): the bounce is a lower-degree (b)-wave and a final probe of the .618 at $2,894 completes a deeper C — the fuel for that path has rotated from a hawkish Fed to DE-ESCALATION plus supply repair (stand-down agreed, EGA's alumina refinery restarted, overseas premiums falling). Bull alternate (15%): the Oman track fails and price accelerates through $3,260 (.382) toward a $3,700+ retest. Invalidation: a weekly close below $2,850 voids the corrective count and re-opens $2,600. Confidence: moderate, improving — the wave structure is constructive, but the news flow now leans against the impulse.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Daily (swing): from the $3,855 top, wave A fell to $3,359 (16-Jun), wave B was sold at the $3,607 spike (22-Jun), and wave C unwound to $3,065 3M / $3,061.5 cash (02-Jul) — a textbook tag of the .50 retracement ($3,077). FIVE consecutive official up-days have followed ($3,087 → $3,113 → $3,127.5 → $3,147 → $3,169), and Thursday's evening session delivered the first impulsive bar of the recovery: +2.29% to $3,210.5 (high $3,213.5), clearing MA5/MA10 with a fresh daily MACD golden cross and bears cutting 1,200 lots — evidence the move off $3,065 is developing motive character. Friday's public references then faded to ~$3,175 on the US–Iran stand-down (officials post Monday). Roadmap: while $3,120–3,150 holds on any pullback, the base case (60%) presses MA20 (~$3,230) then $3,260 (.382), whose clean daily close confirms the correction complete and opens $3,400; a stall at MA20 with de-escalation extending, followed by a daily close below $3,050, re-opens $2,990–3,020 (25%); a decisive $2,950 break exposes $2,894 (.618) (15%). Invalidation for the base: a daily close below $3,050. Confidence: moderate-to-firm — structure improving, headline risk now two-sided.

premium_drivers.t: Hormuz — stand-down after the heaviest 72 hours of the war (08–11 Jul)
premium_drivers.d: Iranian attacks on shipping drew two US strike waves (~85 then ~90 targets, incl. 60+ IRGC attack boats) and Iranian retaliation on US military assets in Gulf states — then both sides agreed to 'stand down for now' and resume talks, with FM Araghchi travelling to Oman Saturday. Transits continued throughout (15 vessels/24h). War-risk freight premia stabilise but lag; a durable stand-down would compress the risk component in Asian and EU premia.

premium_drivers.t: Japan — spot breaks BELOW the decade-high settlement
premium_drivers.d: Q3 MJP settled and Platts-assessed at $395/t (06-Jul, highest in ten years) — yet SMM's 10-Jul weekly shows spot plunging to $355 (from $380), with buyers restocking only immediate needs and traders arbitraging cheap spot against long-term contracts. Korea CIF fell to $319 and Thailand $335. A spot print below settlement one week after it is the classic early signal that Q4 negotiates down.
src: SMM — Weekly Overseas Primary Aluminum Premiums (10-Jul-2026)

premium_drivers.t: US — Section 232 at 50% keeps the floor; demand does the easing
premium_drivers.d: The 50% rate on full customs value (eff. 8-Jun-2026) still makes policy ~2/3 of the US all-in premium (tariff-$ ≈ $1,582 at the 09-Jul cash $3,164). SMM's weekly DDP eased to 109.75¢/lb on off-season demand and H2 restart/new-capacity expectations — the market premium is softening under an unchanged policy umbrella.

premium_drivers.t: Supply repair — EGA restart resets the risk narrative
premium_drivers.d: EGA restarted the 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah alumina refinery (10-Jul; ~50% within days, full capability end-2026) and reports the smelter rebuild AHEAD of schedule (89 cells back, pre-incident output targeted within a year). Europe's floor (6% duty + CBAM + Mozal on care & maintenance) still stands, but the 'Gulf supply gone for a year' premium thesis is now visibly expiring plant by plant.

premium_settlement_src: S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP assessment $395/t (06-Jul-2026) via IndexBox; settlements first reported by Bloomberg via Mining.com (03-Jul)

so_what.line: De-escalation is repricing the tape: Washington and Tehran agreed to stand down and resume talks, Brent gave back the strike-spike, and aluminium — after a FIFTH straight official gain and its strongest close since the June top ($3,210.5, +2.29%) — faded ~1% Friday as the war premium deflates while the supply-repair story (EGA restarting its alumina refinery) accelerates.
points: Exchange: Thursday's officials rose a FIFTH straight session — cash $3,164 (+0.73%) / 3M $3,169 (+0.70%), stock down to 289,225 t and the contango in to -$5 — and the evening session surged +2.29% to $3,210.5, breaking MA5/MA10 with a fresh daily MACD golden cross. Friday's public price references then faded ~1% (to ~$3,175) as oil slid; Friday officials post Monday. $3,260 (.382) is still the upgrade trigger, $3,050 the line.

Geopolitics & macro: after Iran hit US military assets in Gulf states and the US answered with strikes on ~90 more targets (including IRGC attack boats), both sides agreed to 'stand down for now' — Iran's foreign minister travels to Oman this weekend and Trump says talks continue even with the truce 'over'. Brent fell below $76 (round-tripping the whole spike), the US 10Y eased to 4.54% and DXY slipped to ~100.9 — the war floor AND the rates ceiling both softened at once.

Physical: SMM's weekly shows overseas spot premiums broadly LOWER on off-season demand — Japan MJP spot plunged $380→$355 (now below the $395 Q3 settlement), Korea CIF $350→$319, US MW DDP 110.25→109.75¢/lb — while China keeps destocking at a four-year-high pace and EGA restarted its 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah alumina refinery (50% within days) with the smelter rebuild running ahead of schedule.

Watch this week: Monday's LME officials (do Friday's declines confirm a stall at MA20?), US June CPI (~14-Jul), Alcoa Q2 on 16-Jul opening earnings season alongside Rio's Q2 production report, China June trade data, and whether the weekend Oman track holds — a durable stand-down keeps pressure on premiums and the war premium; a breakdown re-bids both.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855

Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.

weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
